eli3: Re-ranking is a second-stage retrieval step that takes the top-k candidates from a fast first-stage retrieval (BM25 or bi-encoder) and scores them more accurately using a slower but more precise model. The first stage optimizes for recall (get all potentially relevant documents), the second stage optimizes for precision (find the most relevant ones among the candidates).

Re-rankers are typically cross-encoders: transformer models that take a (query, document) pair as input and output a single relevance score. Unlike bi-encoders (which embed query and document independently), cross-encoders see both together — the model can assess fine-grained relevance by comparing query terms directly to document terms via attention. This is more accurate but cannot be pre-computed, so it's only practical for a small set of candidates (top-20 to top-100).

The two-stage pipeline (retrieve → re-rank) is the standard architecture for production search and RAG: fast BM25 or bi-encoder retrieval returns top-100 candidates (high recall), cross-encoder re-ranking selects top-5 (high precision). The LLM then generates an answer from these top-5 highly relevant chunks. Popular re-rankers: Cohere Rerank API, cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L6-v2 (open-source), BGE-Reranker.

whenWhy.title: Improving RAG precision with two-stage retrieval
whenWhy.body: Bi-encoder retrieval is fast but imprecise — it embeds query and document independently without comparing them directly. Top-20 bi-encoder results may include irrelevant documents that happened to have similar embeddings. Re-ranking with a cross-encoder: passes each (query, candidate) pair through the re-ranker model, gets a precise relevance score, sorts candidates by score, takes top-5. The LLM context only contains the 5 most relevant chunks — reducing noise, improving answer quality.

whenWhy.title: Handling multi-aspect queries
whenWhy.body: A query like 'What are the side effects of metformin in elderly patients with kidney disease?' has multiple constraints: drug name, patient population, comorbidity. A bi-encoder may retrieve documents about metformin generally, or kidney disease generally, but miss the specific combination. A cross-encoder sees the full query-document pair and scores precisely whether all aspects are addressed. Re-ranking dramatically improves precision for complex, multi-aspect queries.

whenWhy.title: Cohere Rerank and managed re-ranking APIs
whenWhy.body: Cohere Rerank API: send query + list of documents, receive relevance scores. No model hosting required. Latency: ~50ms for top-100 documents. Best for: production RAG where maintaining a self-hosted re-ranker is overhead. Alternative: open-source cross-encoders from sentence-transformers (ms-marco-MiniLM families). Trade-off: Cohere Rerank is faster to deploy but costs per API call; self-hosted is free at scale but requires GPU infrastructure.

deepDiveIntro: Cross-encoder architecture: BERT-style encoder takes [CLS] + query + [SEP] + document + [SEP] as input. [CLS] token representation is passed through a linear layer to produce a single relevance score. Self-attention allows every query token to attend to every document token — captures fine-grained term overlap, semantic relationships, and relevance signals that bi-encoder cosine similarity misses. Training: pointwise (binary relevant/not-relevant), pairwise (relevant > irrelevant), or listwise (optimize ranking metrics like NDCG).

Training data for re-rankers: MS MARCO (Microsoft Machine Reading Comprehension) — 530K (query, relevant passage) pairs from Bing search. Negative sampling: hard negatives from BM25 top results that are not the answer. Models trained on MS MARCO transfer well to other domains. Popular open-source models: cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L6-v2 (fast, 6-layer), cross-encoder/ms-marco-electra-base (accurate). Fine-tune on domain-specific data for best performance.

deepDive.heading: Bi-encoder vs Cross-encoder Comparison
deepDive.body: Bi-encoder: f(q) and f(d) computed independently. Score = cos(f(q), f(d)). Pre-compute f(d) offline → query time: compute f(q) + ANN search. O(1) per query after indexing. Quality: good for general semantic similarity, weaker for fine-grained relevance. Cross-encoder: g(q, d) jointly encodes both. Score = g([q;d]). Cannot pre-compute for unseen queries. Query time: k forward passes through the encoder. O(k) per query. Quality: superior — sees both texts simultaneously, captures exact relevance. In practice: bi-encoder for top-100, cross-encoder re-ranks to top-5. [1]
deepDive.formula: Bi-encoder: cos(f(q), f(d));  Cross-encoder: g([CLS] q [SEP] d [SEP])[0] · w

deepDive.heading: Listwise Re-ranking with LLMs
deepDive.body: Traditional re-ranking: pointwise or pairwise scoring with a dedicated re-ranker model. LLM-based re-ranking: use the LLM itself to re-rank. RankGPT (Sun et al. 2023): prompt the LLM with a listwise ranking instruction — 'Given the query and the following passages, rank them from most to least relevant: [query] [passages]' → LLM outputs a permutation. Surprisingly effective — GPT-4 RankGPT outperforms cross-encoders on TREC benchmarks. Limitation: expensive (LLM inference for re-ranking on every query); sliding window approach needed for >20 candidates. [2]

deepDive.heading: Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF)
deepDive.body: RRF: combine multiple retrieval result lists (BM25 rank, dense rank) without a re-ranker model. RRF score for document d = Σ_r 1/(k + rank_r(d)), where k=60 is a constant and rank_r(d) is document d's rank in retrieval system r. Sum over all retrieval systems r. Advantages: no learned parameters, no additional model, no training data needed. Works because rank is a robust signal — documents ranked high by multiple independent systems are likely relevant. RRF + BM25 + dense retrieval often outperforms either alone. [1]

deepDive.heading: Evaluation: NDCG and MRR
deepDive.body: NDCG@k (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain): measures whether highly relevant documents appear near the top of the ranked list. DCG@k = Σᵢ₌₁ᵏ relᵢ/log₂(i+1) (relevance discounted by position). NDCG = DCG/IDCG (normalized by ideal ranking). MRR (Mean Reciprocal Rank): average of 1/rank_first_relevant across queries — measures how soon the first relevant document appears. Re-ranking goal: maximize NDCG@5 and MRR@10. BEIR benchmark (Thakur et al. 2021): 18 heterogeneous retrieval datasets for out-of-domain evaluation of re-rankers. [1]

takeaways: Re-ranking: first-stage retrieval (recall) → cross-encoder re-ranking (precision) → top-5 to LLM. [1]
Cross-encoder: sees query + document jointly — much more accurate than bi-encoder, but O(k) not O(1). [1]
RRF: combine multiple retrieval lists without a learned model — robust, no training needed. [1]
LLM re-ranking (RankGPT): listwise permutation prompting — strong quality, high inference cost. [2]
NDCG@k and MRR@k: standard metrics for evaluating re-ranking quality. [1]

edgeCases.q: How many candidates should be passed to the re-ranker (top-k)?
edgeCases.a: The trade-off: larger k → higher chance the true relevant document is in the candidate set (recall), but more expensive re-ranking (cross-encoder runs k forward passes). Typical practice: first-stage retrieval top-100, re-rank to top-5 or top-10. Beyond k=100, the marginal recall gain is small (most relevant documents appear in the top-100 for well-trained bi-encoders), while latency increases linearly. For high-precision applications (medical, legal), k=50 first-stage with top-5 re-ranking is a common sweet spot. Monitor recall@k of the first stage — if < 90%, increase k before investing in a better re-ranker. [1]

edgeCases.q: When should you skip re-ranking in a RAG pipeline?
edgeCases.a: Skip re-ranking when: (1) latency budget is tight (< 200ms total) — cross-encoder adds 50–200ms; (2) the knowledge base is small (< 1000 documents) — bi-encoder retrieval alone has high precision at small scale; (3) queries are simple and precise (exact keyword lookup, catalog search) — BM25 or dense retrieval alone is sufficient; (4) cost is constrained — Cohere Rerank API has per-call cost that may not be justified for all traffic. Use re-ranking when: queries are complex/ambiguous, the knowledge base is large and dense, answer quality is more important than latency, and you're observing frequent 'retrieved wrong chunk' errors in RAG outputs. [1]

sources.label: Thakur et al. (2021) — BEIR: A Heterogeneous Benchmark for Zero-shot Evaluation of Information Retrieval Models, NeurIPS 2021
Sun et al. (2023) — Is ChatGPT Good at Search? Investigating Large Language Models as Re-Ranking Agents, EMNLP 2023
